lines.text: Introduction, part one. Why you need game theory.
There is a stubborn myth floating around poker circles that game theory is an advanced topic reserved only for elite players grinding the toughest games in the world.

Andrew Brokos wants to dispel that myth right from the start, because the truth is almost the exact opposite: game theory is for anyone who has ever sat at a poker table and felt genuinely unsure about what the right play was.
If you have never experienced that uncertainty, congratulations — you are apparently the one player in the world who has figured everything out, and the rest of us would love to read your book.
For everyone else, game theory is exactly what you need.
So what is game theory, technically speaking?

The Oxford Dictionaries definition describes it as the branch of mathematics concerned with analyzing strategies in competitive situations where the outcome of what one participant chooses to do depends critically on what other participants choose to do.
Put plainly, game theory gives us a way to build strategies that will perform reasonably well no matter what our opponents decide to do.
For a long time, only a small number of mathematically sophisticated poker players engaged deeply with game theory.

It helped those players grasp certain broad strategic principles, but the sheer complexity of poker meant there was still a great deal of guesswork when it came down to the specifics of any given hand.
What changed everything was the arrival of dedicated solver software and increasingly affordable computing power.
Today, any player willing to do the work can apply game theoretic principles to poker with a degree of rigor and precision that simply was not possible a generation ago.

And even if you have never personally opened a solver, if you have read a poker book or watched an instructional video produced in recent years, you have already been touched by the insights that this software has unlocked.
Game theory is not a cure-all, and Brokos is careful to say so.
It is not the only way to think about poker, but it has become an increasingly indispensable lens.
If you do not have at least a foundational understanding of the game theory that underlies poker, then in an important sense you do not really understand the game.
What you may have instead are strategies that work against certain types of opponents in certain situations you encounter frequently — strategies that were built through trial and error.
That kind of experience-based knowledge can take you a long way, but it has real limits.
You probably feel comfortable in familiar spots and start to struggle when something less common comes up.
And as more of your opponents develop their own game theory knowledge, they will increasingly be able to put you in precisely those difficult, unfamiliar spots.
Here is something important to understand about game theory solver software: it generates strategies of extreme complexity, often mixing many different actions with carefully calibrated frequencies.
But you do not need to memorize those details or reproduce them exactly at the table in order to benefit from studying them.
Unless you are competing at the absolute highest levels of the game, the right goal when studying game theory is not to memorize the precise solution to every specific situation.
Instead, your goal should be to extract broad, transferable principles that can guide you across a wide variety of situations whenever you are unsure what the best play is.
That is exactly what this book is designed to help you do.
Starting from simple, carefully constructed scenarios, it builds toward the most important theoretical concepts that underlie poker strategy.
At each step, you gain new tools you can use immediately and new analytical skills that will help you investigate more complex situations on your own.
You will learn how to ask the right questions, draw the right conclusions from what you observe, and apply these ideas in real time while you are actually playing.
The focus throughout is on trends and principles rather than frequencies and memorized lines.
Along the way, the book may challenge some things you think you already know about poker strategy.

In other cases, it will simply illuminate the theory behind things you already do well, helping you understand not just that something works but why it works and under what conditions it might stop working.
Ultimately, developing a solid grounding in game theory should make you more comfortable and more confident at the poker table.
You will be better equipped to recognize and exploit the mistakes of less-skilled opponents, and you will be able to hold your own against those who are more skilled.
Most importantly, you will feel prepared to handle whatever situation the game throws at you, even situations you have never seen before.
